content.text: distributes every tier of an application across two or more Availability Zones so that an AZ-level failure removes, at most, a fraction of capacity while the remaining AZs absorb the load. Multi-AZ is the foundational HA pattern on AWS — more cost-effective than multi-region for most availability targets.

attrs.alt: Three-tier multi-AZ architecture: public ALB tier, private app tier, and isolated DB tier across us-east-1a, 1b, and 1c

content.text: Multi-AZ architecture rests on a single rule:
no tier may have a single-AZ dependency
. Every resource that holds state or forwards traffic must exist in at least two AZs, connected by a load balancer or replication mechanism that makes the extra copies transparent to clients.
(web, API) scale horizontally. An ALB routes traffic to healthy instances across AZs; losing one AZ simply removes those targets.
(databases, caches) require synchronous or asynchronous replication between AZs. The choice of sync vs. async determines RPO.
(EFS, S3) provides cross-AZ access by design — no per-AZ duplication needed.
(SQS, SNS, EventBridge) is regional and always multi-AZ — no extra configuration required.

Target: SLA 99.99% uptime (≈ 52 min downtime/year). Single-AZ probability of failure over a year is roughly 0.1%. Two AZs bring that to 0.01%; three AZs to 0.001%. Beyond three AZs, the reliability gain is negligible relative to cost.
Tier 1: Public Subnet — ALB and NAT Gateways
The entry point for public traffic. Deploy one
per AZ (the ALB service handles this automatically when you assign subnets in multiple AZs). Add one
so private instances in each AZ have independent outbound internet paths.

# Create an ALB spanning three AZs — nodes are placed in each subnet automatically
ALB_ARN=$(aws elbv2 create-load-balancer \
  --name web-alb \
  --subnets "$PUB_SUBNET_1A" "$PUB_SUBNET_1B" "$PUB_SUBNET_1C" \
  --security-groups "$ALB_SG" \
  --scheme internet-facing \
  --type application \
  --ip-address-type ipv4 \
  --query 'LoadBalancers[0].LoadBalancerArn' --output text)

# Create target group (instances from all three AZs will register here)
TG_ARN=$(aws elbv2 create-target-group \
  --name app-tg \
  --protocol HTTP \
  --port 8080 \
  --vpc-id "$VPC_ID" \
  --health-check-path /health \
  --health-check-interval-seconds 15 \
  --healthy-threshold-count 2 \
  --unhealthy-threshold-count 3 \
  --query 'TargetGroups[0].TargetGroupArn' --output text)

# Attach HTTPS listener
aws elbv2 create-listener \
  --load-balancer-arn "$ALB_ARN" \
  --protocol HTTPS --port 443 \
  --ssl-policy ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06 \
  --certificates CertificateArn="$ACM_CERT_ARN" \
  --default-actions Type=forward,TargetGroupArn="$TG_ARN"
The application tier contains stateless compute: EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling Group, ECS tasks, or Lambda (which is multi-AZ by default). The ASG must reference
so that replacement instances launch in whichever AZs have capacity.

# Launch template for the application tier (no hardcoded AZ)
LT_ID=$(aws ec2 create-launch-template \
  --launch-template-name app-lt \
  --version-description "v1" \
  --launch-template-data '{
    "ImageId": "ami-0abcdef1234567890",
    "InstanceType": "m5.large",
    "SecurityGroupIds": ["'"$APP_SG"'"],
    "IamInstanceProfile": {"Arn": "'"$APP_INSTANCE_PROFILE"'"},
    "UserData": "'"$(base64 -w0 /tmp/userdata.sh)"'"
  }' --query 'LaunchTemplate.LaunchTemplateId' --output text)

# ASG across all three private subnets
aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group \
  --auto-scaling-group-name app-asg \
  --launch-template LaunchTemplateId="$LT_ID",Version=1 \
  --min-size 3 \
  --max-size 12 \
  --desired-capacity 6 \
  --vpc-zone-identifier "$PRIV_SUBNET_1A,$PRIV_SUBNET_1B,$PRIV_SUBNET_1C" \
  --target-group-arns "$TG_ARN" \
  --health-check-type ELB \
  --health-check-grace-period 120 \
  --availability-zone-distribution AZAllocationStrategy=balanced

# Target-tracking scaling: keep CPU at 60%
aws autoscaling put-scaling-policy \
  --auto-scaling-group-name app-asg \
  --policy-name cpu-tracking \
  --policy-type TargetTrackingScaling \
  --target-tracking-configuration '{
    "PredefinedMetricSpecification": {"PredefinedMetricType": "ASGAverageCPUUtilization"},
    "TargetValue": 60
  }'
The database tier holds the most critical state. The correct service choice depends on your RPO (Recovery Point Objective) and throughput requirements:
Synchronous replication to standby in another AZ
Primary in one AZ; replica in another; auto-failover
Cache layer; must tolerate small data loss

# Create RDS Multi-AZ MySQL in the isolated subnets
# Step 1: DB subnet group covering all three AZs
aws rds create-db-subnet-group \
  --db-subnet-group-name prod-db-subnets \
  --db-subnet-group-description "Multi-AZ DB subnets" \
  --subnet-ids "$ISOL_SUBNET_1A" "$ISOL_SUBNET_1B" "$ISOL_SUBNET_1C"

# Step 2: Create Multi-AZ RDS instance
aws rds create-db-instance \
  --db-instance-identifier prod-mysql \
  --db-instance-class db.m5.large \
  --engine mysql --engine-version 8.0 \
  --master-username admin \
  --master-user-password "$(aws secretsmanager get-secret-value --secret-id prod/db/admin --query SecretString --output text | jq -r .password)" \
  --allocated-storage 100 --storage-type gp3 \
  --multi-az \
  --db-subnet-group-name prod-db-subnets \
  --vpc-security-group-ids "$DB_SG" \
  --backup-retention-period 7 \
  --deletion-protection \
  --enabled-cloudwatch-logs-exports '["error","general","slowquery"]'

# Step 3: Confirm standby AZ
aws rds describe-db-instances \
  --db-instance-identifier prod-mysql \
  --query 'DBInstances[0].{Primary:AvailabilityZone,Standby:SecondaryAvailabilityZone,MultiAZ:MultiAZ}'
Cache clusters require separate configuration for Multi-AZ. A Redis cluster with
promotes a replica to primary within 30–60 s if the primary node fails.

# Create an ElastiCache Redis replication group with one primary and two replicas
# across all three AZs
aws elasticache create-replication-group \
  --replication-group-id prod-redis \
  --replication-group-description "Multi-AZ Redis" \
  --num-cache-clusters 3 \
  --cache-node-type cache.m5.large \
  --engine redis --engine-version 7.0 \
  --automatic-failover-enabled \
  --multi-az-enabled \
  --cache-subnet-group-name prod-cache-subnets \
  --preferred-cache-cluster-azs us-east-1a us-east-1b us-east-1c \
  --security-group-ids "$CACHE_SG" \
  --at-rest-encryption-enabled \
  --transit-encryption-enabled

# Verify AZ distribution
aws elasticache describe-replication-groups \
  --replication-group-id prod-redis \
  --query 'ReplicationGroups[0].NodeGroups[0].NodeGroupMembers[*].{Role:CurrentRole,AZ:PreferredAvailabilityZone}'
EFS: Shared Storage Without AZ Boundaries

. All mount targets share the same file system — data written via one mount target is immediately visible via all others. This makes EFS ideal for shared configuration, machine-learning datasets, or user-uploaded content that must be accessible from any AZ.

# Create EFS with mount targets in all three AZs
FS_ID=$(aws efs create-file-system \
  --creation-token "prod-efs-$(date +%s)" \
  --performance-mode generalPurpose \
  --throughput-mode elastic \
  --encrypted \
  --tags Key=Name,Value=prod-data Key=Env,Value=prod \
  --query 'FileSystemId' --output text)

# Create one mount target per isolated subnet
for SUBNET in "$ISOL_SUBNET_1A" "$ISOL_SUBNET_1B" "$ISOL_SUBNET_1C"; do
  aws efs create-mount-target \
    --file-system-id "$FS_ID" \
    --subnet-id "$SUBNET" \
    --security-groups "$EFS_SG"
done

# Mount on an EC2 instance using NFS (EFS mount helper resolves AZ-local target automatically)
# Install: sudo yum install -y amazon-efs-utils
# sudo mount -t efs -o tls,iam "$FS_ID":/ /data
ECS Fargate places tasks across AZs automatically when the service's
network configuration references subnets in multiple AZs
strategy (ENABLED) redistributes tasks after an AZ recovers to maintain the desired spread.

# ECS Fargate service with AZ spread strategy
aws ecs create-service \
  --cluster prod-cluster \
  --service-name web-api \
  --task-definition web-api:5 \
  --desired-count 6 \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --platform-version LATEST \
  --network-configuration '{
    "awsvpcConfiguration": {
      "subnets": ["'"$PRIV_SUBNET_1A"'", "'"$PRIV_SUBNET_1B"'", "'"$PRIV_SUBNET_1C"'"],
      "securityGroups": ["'"$APP_SG"'"],
      "assignPublicIp": "DISABLED"
    }
  }' \
  --load-balancers targetGroupArn="$TG_ARN",containerName=web,containerPort=8080 \
  --deployment-configuration maximumPercent=200,minimumHealthyPercent=50 \
  --availability-zone-rebalancing ENABLED \
  --placement-strategy type=spread,field=attribute:ecs.availability-zone

# Verify AZ distribution of running tasks
aws ecs list-tasks --cluster prod-cluster --service-name web-api --query 'taskArns' | \
  xargs -I{} aws ecs describe-tasks --cluster prod-cluster --tasks {} \
  --query 'tasks[*].{AZ:availabilityZone,Status:lastStatus}' --output table
SQS and SNS: Regional by Default
SQS queues and SNS topics are
— they replicate messages across multiple AZs automatically. Your producers and consumers do not need to be AZ-aware. The key multi-AZ considerations are:
: Always call the regional queue endpoint; route through the VPC endpoint (Interface type) to keep traffic off the public internet.
: Deploy consumers in each AZ so that an AZ failure only reduces consumer throughput, not availability.
: Maintain strict ordering and exactly-once delivery with the same multi-AZ durability as standard queues.
: A DLQ is also regional and multi-AZ. Failed messages land there regardless of which AZ's consumer processed them.

# Create SQS queue with a DLQ — both regional (no AZ selection needed)
DLQ_ARN=$(aws sqs create-queue \
  --queue-name prod-jobs-dlq \
  --attributes MessageRetentionPeriod=1209600 \
  --query 'QueueUrl' --output text | \
  xargs -I{} aws sqs get-queue-attributes --queue-url {} \
  --attribute-names QueueArn --query 'Attributes.QueueArn' --output text)

aws sqs create-queue \
  --queue-name prod-jobs \
  --attributes '{
    "VisibilityTimeout": "60",
    "MessageRetentionPeriod": "86400",
    "RedrivePolicy": "{"deadLetterTargetArn":"'"$DLQ_ARN"'","maxReceiveCount":"5"}"
  }'

# Consumers run in each AZ (ECS tasks or Lambda) — SQS distributes messages automatically
Infrastructure as Code: Full Multi-AZ VPC

CloudFormation or Terraform lets you define the entire multi-AZ structure reproducibly. The following Terraform snippet creates the VPC, subnets, NAT Gateways, and route tables following the three-AZ pattern:
A multi-AZ architecture is only trustworthy after it has survived an AZ failure in
. The following test matrix covers each tier:
All EC2/ECS in one AZ terminated
Error rate < 1% during transition; recovery < 60 s
App reconnects via CNAME; write gap < 120 s
Clients reconnect to promoted replica; cold cache hit rate spikes then recovers
Remove route to NAT GW in one AZ
Instances in that AZ lose internet; others unaffected
Block traffic from one mount target
NFS operations in that AZ fail; others continue
Three-AZ deployments introduce several new cost lines. These controls reduce the bill without sacrificing availability:
Route S3 and DynamoDB via VPC Endpoints (free gateway endpoints)
Enable AZ affinity in ECS Service Connect; use EKS topology hints
Use Multi-AZ Cluster (adds read scalability) or Aurora Serverless v2
Single replica if RPO of seconds is acceptable; use Graviton (up to 20% cheaper)
No charge per mount target; pay per GB stored + transfer
Lifecycle policy: move to EFS-IA after 30 days (68% cheaper)

# Add VPC Gateway Endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB (free; avoids NAT GW data charges)
aws ec2 create-vpc-endpoint \
  --vpc-id "$VPC_ID" \
  --service-name com.amazonaws.us-east-1.s3 \
  --route-table-ids "$RT_PRIV_1A" "$RT_PRIV_1B" "$RT_PRIV_1C" \
  --vpc-endpoint-type Gateway

aws ec2 create-vpc-endpoint \
  --vpc-id "$VPC_ID" \
  --service-name com.amazonaws.us-east-1.dynamodb \
  --route-table-ids "$RT_PRIV_1A" "$RT_PRIV_1B" "$RT_PRIV_1C" \
  --vpc-endpoint-type Gateway

# EFS Lifecycle policy: move to Infrequent Access after 30 days
aws efs put-lifecycle-configuration \
  --file-system-id "$FS_ID" \
  --lifecycle-policies TransitionToIA=AFTER_30_DAYS,TransitionToPrimaryStorageClass=AFTER_1_ACCESS
Use this checklist before declaring an application production-ready:
ASG VPCZoneIdentifier lists subnets in ≥ 2 AZs
or Aurora has readers in separate AZs.
: Each private subnet's route table points to its local NAT GW.
EFS mount targets exist in each AZ
: Each tier survived a simulated AZ outage with error rate below SLA threshold.
: No rule hard-codes a single AZ CIDR or AZ endpoint.
Multi-AZ is not a feature to enable
— it is an architectural discipline applied independently to every tier (compute, database, cache, storage, networking).
(EC2 ASG, ECS Fargate, Lambda) are multi-AZ by default when subnets in multiple AZs are provided; stateful tiers require explicit configuration.
The ALB + ASG + RDS Multi-AZ trinity
covers most web-application HA requirements and can achieve RTO < 120 s and RPO = 0.
: NAT Gateways, cross-AZ transfer, and DB standbys all add up; use VPC endpoints, AZ-affinity routing, and EFS lifecycle policies to control the bill.
: Use AWS FIS or manual subnet routing changes in staging to confirm that your multi-AZ design works end-to-end before a real outage reveals the gap.
